content.text: The Handoff Problem: Why Research Findings Don't Become Requirements

User research for AI products routinely produces rich findings — coded transcripts, trust conditions, edge case catalogs, scope negotiation briefs — that do not survive the transition to engineering. The findings exist in a research report that engineers read once and product managers partially summarise in a planning document. Three months later, a critical trust condition from an interview becomes a post-launch bug. An anti-goal documented in a research report reappears as an unintended system behaviour because no one translated it into a system prompt constraint.

This topic covers the structural problem with the research-to-requirements handoff and the specific artifacts and practices that prevent research findings from being lost. The core principle is that traceability must be bidirectional: every requirement should point back to a specific research insight, and every significant research insight should point forward to a specific requirement, test case, or architectural decision.
The Handoff Flow: Five Stages

attrs.alt: Research to requirements handoff flow showing five stages: Research outputs, Synthesis artifacts, Requirement types, Backlog tickets, and Traceability links
attrs.title: Research to requirements handoff flow showing five stages: Research outputs, Synthesis artifacts, Requirement types, Backlog tickets, and Traceability links

content.text: The handoff is not a single event but a translation pipeline with five stages. Losing traceability at any stage allows requirements to drift from their research basis. Adding a requirement without a research source, or removing a requirement without checking its source, both indicate the pipeline has broken.
Stage 1 — Research: raw outputs from interviews, trust calibration sessions, and edge case collection
Stage 2 — Synthesis: structured insight cards, opportunity matrices, trust requirement maps
Stage 3 — Requirements: typed requirements (user stories, NFRs, acceptance criteria, anti-goals) derived from synthesis artifacts
Stage 4 — Backlog: prioritised tickets in the project management system, linked to requirements
Stage 5 — Traceability: bidirectional links from tickets to insights and from insights to tickets
Insight Cards: The Atomic Unit of Research-to-Requirements Translation
Why Insight Cards Work

An insight card is a single structured document that captures one finding from user research with enough context to justify a specific requirement. The key design principle is that an insight card must be independently legible: an engineer reading the card six months after the study should be able to understand what participants said, why it matters, and what requirement it generated — without reading the full research report.

Insight cards prevent the most common translation failure: a finding that is accurate but untethered. 'Users want source attribution' is a finding. 'Four participants (three senior associates and one partner) said they would not accept an AI finding without seeing the specific clause it came from, with two participants saying they would read the full contract anyway if source attribution was missing' is an insight card. The second version generates a specific, measurable, prioritised requirement. The first version generates a vague feature wish.
Insight Card Format: Full YAML Specification

Store insight cards in version control alongside requirements and code. The YAML format makes them diff-able — you can see exactly what changed between research rounds — and parseable by the traceability tools described later in this topic.

# insight-card-INS-047.yaml
# Format: research-to-requirements insight card
id: INS-047
source:
  study: "Contract Review AI — Discovery Round 1"
  sessions: ["P-03", "P-07", "P-11", "P-14"]
  date_range: "2025-03-10 to 2025-03-18"

observation:
  summary: "Participants expect to see which sentences in the source document the AI used to generate a finding before they will accept the finding without independent review."
  verbatim_quotes:
    - participant: P-03
      role: senior_associate
      text: "I need to be able to click on the finding and see exactly what clause it came from. Otherwise I'm going to read the whole contract anyway."
    - participant: P-07
      role: partner
      text: "If I can't see the source, it's not a draft I can sign off on — it's a guess."
    - participant: P-11
      role: paralegal
      text: "I always go back and verify, but if it showed me the line number I could just glance instead of hunting."
  frequency: 4  # sessions where this pattern appeared
  strength: high  # high | medium | low

interpretation:
  insight: "Source attribution is a prerequisite for workflow integration, not a nice-to-have feature. Without it, users perform a full manual review regardless of AI output, eliminating time savings."
  trust_implication: "Under-trust cohort (partners) and efficiency-seekers (paralegals) both require this — suggesting it is a universal need, not role-specific."

requirements_generated:
  - type: user_story
    id: US-019
    text: "As a contract reviewer, I want each AI finding to link to the specific clause(s) that support it, so that I can verify the finding without reading the full document."
    acceptance_criteria:
      - "Each finding card displays the verbatim source text highlighted in the document viewer"
      - "Source text is scroll-synced: clicking the finding scrolls the document to the source location"
      - "If AI confidence < 0.70, source link is labelled 'uncertain — verify manually'"

  - type: nfr
    id: NFR-012
    category: transparency
    statement: "The system must provide source attribution for every finding: which document section and which sentences were used to generate the finding."
    measurability: "100% of findings in the output schema must carry a non-empty source_spans field"

  - type: anti_goal
    id: AG-004
    statement: "The system must not present a finding without a source attribution, even if the source extraction model cannot identify a specific span."
    fallback: "If source extraction fails, the finding is suppressed and logged as 'unattributable finding' for model improvement."

traceability:
  insight_id: INS-047
  linked_requirements: ["US-019", "NFR-012", "AG-004"]
  linked_test_cases: ["TC-089", "TC-090"]
  linked_eval_criteria: ["EVAL-source-attribution-coverage"]
The Research Handoff Brief
Purpose and Audience

The research handoff brief is a single document that the research team hands to product and engineering at the conclusion of a discovery round. It is not a research report (which goes to stakeholders and documents methodology) — it is an operational document that tells engineering what to build in the first sprint, what the hard constraints are, and what success looks like. Its audience is the product manager, tech lead, QA lead, and ML engineer, not the executive sponsor.

The brief is structured around four required sections: prioritised capabilities (with frequency counts and sprint targets), non-functional requirements (each traceable to an insight), anti-goals (each with an enforcement mechanism), and edge cases as acceptance criteria (each with a corresponding test case ID). A brief that is missing any of these sections is incomplete.
Research Handoff Brief: Full YAML Template

# research-handoff-brief-v1.yaml
# Generated from: Contract Review AI — Discovery Round 1 (12 participants, 4 roles)
# Handoff date: 2025-03-25
# Handoff owners: Research → Product (Alice Chen), Engineering (Raj Patel), QA (Maya Santos)

prioritised_capabilities:
  # Frequency threshold: ≥ 4 participant sessions
  - id: CAP-001
    title: "Source-attributed finding extraction"
    description: "Identify and surface contractual findings with direct source clause links"
    participant_frequency: 12
    insight_ids: ["INS-047", "INS-051"]
    sprint_target: 1
    owner: engineering

  - id: CAP-002
    title: "Risk tier classification"
    description: "Classify each finding as High / Medium / Low risk with explanation"
    participant_frequency: 9
    insight_ids: ["INS-033", "INS-044"]
    sprint_target: 1
    owner: engineering

  - id: CAP-003
    title: "Comparison to standard positions"
    description: "Flag deviation from firm's standard clause positions library"
    participant_frequency: 7
    insight_ids: ["INS-058"]
    sprint_target: 2
    owner: engineering

non_functional_requirements:
  - id: NFR-012
    category: transparency
    statement: "100% of findings must carry source_spans referencing verbatim document text"
    source_insight: INS-047
    owner: engineering

  - id: NFR-013
    category: latency
    statement: "Full contract analysis (≤ 150 pages) must complete within 90 seconds P95"
    source_insight: INS-039
    owner: engineering

  - id: NFR-014
    category: accuracy
    statement: "Recall on material adverse change clauses ≥ 0.92 on internal benchmark set"
    source_insight: INS-031
    owner: ml_team

  - id: NFR-015
    category: cost
    statement: "Per-contract API cost must not exceed $0.40 at P95 document length"
    source_insight: INS-062
    owner: engineering

anti_goals:
  - id: AG-001
    statement: "The system must never submit, save, or send any document or finding without explicit user action"
    source_insight: INS-022
    enforcement: system_prompt_hard_constraint

  - id: AG-002
    statement: "The system must never present risk classifications as legal advice"
    source_insight: INS-028
    enforcement: ui_disclaimer_plus_system_prompt

  - id: AG-003
    statement: "The system must never analyse documents that contain non-English clauses without surfacing a language-limitation warning"
    source_insight: INS-055
    enforcement: pre_analysis_language_detection

  - id: AG-004
    statement: "The system must not present a finding without source attribution"
    source_insight: INS-047
    enforcement: schema_validation_pre_render

edge_cases_as_acceptance_criteria:
  - id: EC-001
    description: "Contract contains two parties with identical names in different capacities"
    severity: blocker
    source_insight: INS-041
    acceptance_criterion: "System correctly attributes obligations to the correct party in all cases on the benchmark set; ambiguous attributions are flagged with a warning"
    test_case: TC-071

  - id: EC-002
    description: "Force majeure clause uses non-standard language not in training distribution"
    severity: high
    source_insight: INS-048
    acceptance_criterion: "System either correctly identifies the clause or explicitly flags 'clause type uncertain — manual review required'; must not silently misclassify"
    test_case: TC-083

  - id: EC-003
    description: "Exhibit or schedule contradicts the body clause"
    severity: high
    source_insight: INS-052
    acceptance_criterion: "System surfaces both the body clause and the exhibit clause as separate findings when their content conflicts"
    test_case: TC-091

success_definition:
  north_star: "A senior associate completes initial contract review in < 30 minutes (baseline: 90 minutes)"
  guardrail_metrics:
    - metric: "Material clause miss rate in production sample audits"
      threshold: "< 5%"
    - metric: "User override rate on High-risk findings"
      threshold: "< 25% (high override = AI not matching firm risk positions)"
    - metric: "Source attribution coverage"
      threshold: "100% of findings"
  review_cycle: "30-day post-launch retrospective against guardrail metrics"
Traceability Implementation: Python
Why Traceability Must Be Enforced Programmatically

Manual traceability — a spreadsheet mapping requirements to research findings — decays. Engineers add requirements for technical reasons without a research source. Product managers remove requirements without checking their research basis. By the third sprint, the traceability document is outdated and nobody maintains it. Programmatic traceability enforcement makes this decay visible: a CI check that runs on every PR and fails when orphaned requirements (no linked insight) or unactioned insights (no generated requirement) are detected.
Backlog Generation: Converting the Brief to Jira Tickets

The final stage of the handoff is converting the brief's structured content into project management tickets with the research context embedded in the ticket description. Embedding context directly in tickets prevents the second-most common failure mode: the insight card exists, the requirement was created, but the engineer implementing the ticket has no idea why the requirement was specified the way it was and simplifies it without realising the simplification violates a participant's explicit trust condition.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class JiraTicket:
    issue_type: str  # Story | Task | Bug | Sub-task
    summary: str
    description: str
    labels: list[str]
    sprint: Optional[int]
    components: list[str]
    custom_fields: dict


def capability_to_story(cap: dict, insight_summary: str) -> JiraTicket:
    # Converts a capability entry from the handoff brief to a Jira Story
    ac_lines = []
    for ac in cap.get("acceptance_criteria", []):
        ac_lines.append(f"* {ac}")
    ac_text = "\n".join(ac_lines) if ac_lines else "See linked insight card."

    return JiraTicket(
        issue_type="Story",
        summary=cap["title"],
        description=(
            f"h3. User Story\n"
            f"{cap.get('description', '')}\n\n"
            f"h3. Research Basis\n"
            f"Insight: {', '.join(cap.get('insight_ids', []))}\n"
            f"Participant frequency: {cap.get('participant_frequency', 'N/A')} sessions\n"
            f"Insight summary: {insight_summary}\n\n"
            f"h3. Acceptance Criteria\n"
            f"{ac_text}"
        ),
        labels=["ai-feature", "research-backed", f"sprint-{cap.get('sprint_target', 'tbd')}"],
        sprint=cap.get("sprint_target"),
        components=[cap.get("owner", "engineering")],
        custom_fields={
            "research_insight_ids": cap.get("insight_ids", []),
            "participant_frequency": cap.get("participant_frequency"),
        },
    )


def nfr_to_task(nfr: dict) -> JiraTicket:
    # Converts an NFR entry to a Jira Task
    return JiraTicket(
        issue_type="Task",
        summary=f"[NFR] {nfr['category'].upper()}: {nfr['id']}",
        description=(
            f"h3. Non-Functional Requirement\n"
            f"{nfr['statement']}\n\n"
            f"h3. Measurability\n"
            f"{nfr.get('measurability', 'See handoff brief.')}\n\n"
            f"h3. Research Source\n"
            f"Insight: {nfr.get('source_insight', 'N/A')}\n"
            f"Owner: {nfr.get('owner', 'engineering')}"
        ),
        labels=["nfr", f"nfr-{nfr['category']}", "research-backed"],
        sprint=None,  # NFRs are not sprint-targeted; they constrain all sprints
        components=[nfr.get("owner", "engineering")],
        custom_fields={
            "nfr_category": nfr["category"],
            "source_insight": nfr.get("source_insight"),
        },
    )


def anti_goal_to_constraint_task(ag: dict) -> JiraTicket:
    # Converts an anti-goal to a Jira Task for the system prompt / guardrail workstream
    return JiraTicket(
        issue_type="Task",
        summary=f"[CONSTRAINT] {ag['id']}: encode anti-goal in system prompt and eval suite",
        description=(
            f"h3. Anti-Goal Statement\n"
            f"{ag['statement']}\n\n"
            f"h3. Enforcement Mechanism\n"
            f"{ag.get('enforcement', 'See handoff brief.')}\n\n"
            f"h3. Research Source\n"
            f"Insight: {ag.get('source_insight', 'N/A')}\n\n"
            f"h3. Acceptance Criteria\n"
            f"* System prompt contains explicit instruction prohibiting this behaviour\n"
            f"* Eval suite contains ≥ 5 red-teaming cases targeting this constraint\n"
            f"* QA sign-off required before sprint completion"
        ),
        labels=["constraint", "system-prompt", "eval-required", "research-backed"],
        sprint=1,  # Anti-goals are Sprint 1 items: they must be in from day one
        components=["engineering", "ml_team"],
        custom_fields={
            "constraint_type": "anti_goal",
            "source_insight": ag.get("source_insight"),
            "enforcement": ag.get("enforcement"),
        },
    )


def generate_backlog(brief: dict) -> list[JiraTicket]:
    tickets = []
    for cap in brief.get("prioritised_capabilities", []):
        tickets.append(capability_to_story(cap, insight_summary="See insight card."))
    for nfr in brief.get("non_functional_requirements", []):
        tickets.append(nfr_to_task(nfr))
    for ag in brief.get("anti_goals", []):
        tickets.append(anti_goal_to_constraint_task(ag))
    return tickets


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    import yaml

    brief_path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "research-handoff-brief-v1.yaml"
    with open(brief_path) as f:
        brief = yaml.safe_load(f)

    tickets = generate_backlog(brief)
    output = [asdict(t) for t in tickets]
    print(json.dumps(output, indent=2))
    print(f"Generated {len(output)} Jira tickets from handoff brief.")
Requirement Type Mapping
Each Research Artifact Type Has a Corresponding Requirement Type

The mapping from research artifact to requirement type is not arbitrary — it is structurally determined by what the artifact captures and what engineering action it requires. Capability requests capture what participants want the system to do; they become user stories. Trust conditions capture prerequisites for adoption; they become NFRs in the transparency, safety, or reliability categories. Edge cases capture situations where the system might fail; they become acceptance criteria and QA test cases. Anti-goals capture actions the system must never take; they become hard constraints in the system prompt and entries in the evaluation suite.
Research Artifact
Requirement Type
Engineering Action
Verification Method
Capability request (≥ threshold frequency)
User story
Feature implementation
Acceptance test in QA suite
Trust condition
Non-functional requirement (transparency, safety)
UI component or system constraint
NFR test; automated or audit-based
Edge case (blocker/high)
Acceptance criterion
Edge case handling in code; test fixture
Seeded error test in QA suite
Anti-goal
Hard constraint
System prompt instruction; guardrail evaluation
Red-teaming eval; system prompt review
Success indicator
Outcome metric
Instrumentation and dashboard
Metric present in launch readiness review
Blocker concern
Risk entry
Risk mitigation plan or go/no-go criterion
Risk review before launch
Anti-Goals: The Most Frequently Dropped Requirement Type
Why Anti-Goals Get Lost

Anti-goals are the most frequently lost requirement type in AI product development. They are dropped at three points: first, in the translation from research insight to requirement (the anti-goal is documented in a research report but never typed as a specific requirement); second, in the handoff to engineering (the requirement exists but no one is responsible for encoding it in the system prompt); third, during implementation (an engineer is not aware the anti-goal exists because it is not linked to any of the tickets they are working on).

The structural fix is to treat anti-goals as Sprint 1 blockers: they must be in the system prompt and validated by the eval suite before any other Sprint 1 capability is considered complete. This forces the team to resolve anti-goal encoding early, when changing the system prompt is cheap, rather than late, when established behaviour is harder to modify.
Every anti-goal must have an enforcement mechanism: system prompt instruction, UI guardrail, schema validation, or pre-flight check
Every anti-goal must have at least five red-teaming eval cases that attempt to trigger the prohibited behaviour
Anti-goals must be reviewed by the research team before sprint demo to confirm the enforcement matches the original participant intent
Anti-goal violations discovered in QA are Sprint 1 blockers — they do not carry over to Sprint 2
Anti-goals must be re-verified after every system prompt change, even if the change was for an unrelated reason
Handling Research Gaps and Conflicts During Handoff
When Insights Conflict with Engineering Constraints

Research insights occasionally conflict with engineering constraints: participants require a source attribution feature that the selected model architecture cannot reliably produce, or a trust condition requires latency well below what the infrastructure can provide. These conflicts must be surfaced and resolved in the handoff meeting, not discovered silently during implementation.

The resolution process has a specific structure. First, confirm the constraint is real — engineering feasibility estimates made before architecture investigation are frequently pessimistic. Second, if the constraint is confirmed, go back to the research insight and determine which aspect of the participant need the constraint prevents meeting. Third, design a partial solution: if real-time source attribution is infeasible, can sources be shown in a post-processing step? Document the decision and the rationale in the insight card, linking it to the ADR (architectural decision record) that captures the constraint.
Never silently drop a research insight because of an engineering constraint — document the conflict and the resolution
If a trust condition cannot be met, the launch readiness decision must acknowledge the gap explicitly
Partial solutions must be validated with at least one participant from the research cohort before assuming they satisfy the original need
Conflicts between insights from different participant roles should be resolved by defining scope: whose trust condition takes priority for this sprint?
All insight-to-requirement conflicts must be documented in the handoff brief appendix for future research rounds
Running the Handoff Meeting
Roles and Agenda

The handoff meeting is a 90-minute working session, not a presentation. The research team's role is to explain the findings; the product manager's role is to confirm priorities and sprint targets; the tech lead's role is to surface feasibility constraints; the QA lead's role is to confirm that edge cases and anti-goals generate testable criteria; the ML engineer's role is to confirm that NFRs for accuracy and latency are achievable by the current model.
Pre-read (before meeting): distribute the handoff brief and insight cards 48 hours in advance with explicit instruction to read, not skim
Capability review (30 min): walk through each prioritised capability; confirm sprint targets; surface and document feasibility constraints
NFR and anti-goal review (20 min): confirm each NFR has an owner and a measurability definition; confirm each anti-goal has an enforcement mechanism and eval cases assigned
Edge case triage (20 min): confirm severity classification for each edge case; assign QA test cases; confirm which edge cases are sprint blockers
Traceability check (10 min): run the coverage report from the traceability tool; resolve any orphaned requirements or unactioned insights before closing the meeting
Action items (10 min): document who creates which tickets, by when; confirm research team availability for clarification questions during sprint
Key Takeaways

Research findings become requirements only if the translation is explicit and typed: capability requests become user stories, trust conditions become NFRs, edge cases become acceptance criteria, anti-goals become system prompt constraints.

Insight cards are the atomic unit: each one links a specific observation (with verbatim quotes and participant frequency) to the specific requirements it generated, creating bidirectional traceability.
Anti-goals are Sprint 1 blockers: they must be in the system prompt, validated by red-teaming eval cases, and reviewed by the research team before the sprint closes.
Programmatic traceability enforcement — a CI check that fails on orphaned requirements or unactioned insights — prevents the decay that makes manual traceability ineffective after the first sprint.

The handoff brief is an operational document, not a research report: its audience is the tech lead, product manager, and QA lead, and its purpose is to define sprint scope, constraints, and success criteria — not to document methodology.
